coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots doped thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane on macroporous ceramic hollow fiber support via one-step interfacial polymerization
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yu-Zhe
Shareef, Usman
Xu, Ji-Peng
Xu, Zhen-Liang
Li, Ping-Ping
Li, Yu-Xuan
Li, Ping
Gao, Peng
Zhang, Xin
Xu, Sun-Jie
coredata.dc:description: The amino (NH2) modified carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) doped thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes were prepared on the macroporous ceramic hollow fiber (CHF) substrate with a pore size of about 300 nm through one-step interfacial polymerization (IP) process. The abundant hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups of aqueous phase additives (N-CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)) provide attachment sites for ethylenediamine (EDA) to build a modified membrane structure. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation was employed to study the diffusion process in IP. The results show that the addition of N-CDs reduced the EDA diffusion coefficient significantly from (4.58 ± 0.50) × 10−5 cm2/s to (1.44 ± 0.10) × 10−5 cm2/s. The wrinkled and ultra-thin polyamide (PA) layer with a thickness of 28 ± 3 nm was observed by the field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and dynamic contact angle were used to characterize the chemical structure and hydrophilicity. The effect of additives concentration on pervaporation (PV) performance for ethanol (EtOH) dehydration has been investigated systematically. The resultant membrane exhibited excellent performance with the total permeation flux of 3.15 kg·m−2·h−1 and separation factor of 1127. Besides, there was no significant performance decline during the 160 h long-term stability test, indicating that the membranes own the potential application in industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Interfacial polymerization
Pervaporation
Ceramic hollow fiber
Polyvinyl alcohol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design of an aqueous solution for IP
EtOH dehydration performance of N-CDsPCHFPV nanocomposite membranes
Effect of operating conditions on EtOH dehydration performance
PV performance of N-CDsPCHFPV membrane in long-term operation
Comparison of EtOH dehydration performance with state-of-the-art literature

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of N-CDs synthesis route.
Characterization of as-synthesized N-CDs: (a) high-resolution TEM image; (b) particle size distribution; (c) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs; (d) XRD spectrum of N-CDs.
Schematic diagram of the IP reaction mechanism: without N-CDs and PVA (Left); adding N-CDs and PVA (Right).
MD simulation: (a) concentration distribution of EDA in N-C/P/E system; (b) construction of MD for the IP process; (c) MD simulation of EDA diffusion coefficient in three systems.

SEM cross-section images (Left) and surface images (Right) of (a) ceramic hollow fiber (CHF) support; (b) PV composite membrane without aqueous phase additive; (c–d) PVA was added to the IP process; (e–f) PVA and N-CDs were added to the IP process.
FT-IR spectra of CDs, PCHFPV, and N-CDsPCHFPV membrane.
Results of XPS analyses: (a) wide-scan XPS spectra and high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of (b) CHFPV; (c) P0.1CHFPV; (d) N-CDs500P0.1CHFPV membrane surface.
Dynamic water contact angle of CHF, CHFPV, and N-CDsPCHFPV membranes EtOH Dehydration Performance of N-CDsPCHFPV Membranes.
Effect of PVA (Left) and N-CDs (Right) concentration on PV dehydration performance of PCHFPV membranes for 90/10 wt% EtOH/water mixtures at 60 °C.

Effect of operating temperature on EtOH dehydration performance of N-CDs500P0.1CHFPV membrane for 90/10 wt% EtOH/water solution: (a) total flux and separation factor; (b) partial flux of water and EtOH; (c) apparent activation energy fitting of water and EtOH; (d) PSI with different feed temperature.
Long-term PV dehydration performance test of N-CDs500P0.1CHFPV membrane for 90/10 wt% EtOH/water mixtures at 60 °C: total flux and separation factor.
Comparison with state-of-the-art literature: EtOH dehydration performance.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots doped thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane on macroporous ceramic hollow fiber support via one-step interfacial polymerization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, Membrane Science and Engineering R&D Lab, Chemical Engineering Research Center, School of Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, 130 Meilong Road, Shanghai 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering
Membrane Science and Engineering R&D Lab
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, Membrane Science and Engineering R&D Lab, Chemical Engineering Research Center, School of Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, 130 Meilong Road, Shanghai 200237, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The amino (NH2) modified carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) doped thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes were prepared on the macroporous ceramic hollow fiber (CHF) substrate with a pore size of about 300 nm through one-step interfacial polymerization (IP) process. The abundant hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups of aqueous phase additives (N-CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)) provide attachment sites for ethylenediamine (EDA) to build a modified membrane structure. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation was employed to study the diffusion process in IP. The results show that the addition of N-CDs reduced the EDA diffusion coefficient significantly from (4.58 ± 0.50) × 10−5 cm2/s to (1.44 ± 0.10) × 10−5 cm2/s. The wrinkled and ultra-thin polyamide (PA) layer with a thickness of 28 ± 3 nm was observed by the field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and dynamic contact angle were used to characterize the chemical structure and hydrophilicity. The effect of additives concentration on pervaporation (PV) performance for ethanol (EtOH) dehydration has been investigated systematically. The resultant membrane exhibited excellent performance with the total permeation flux of 3.15 kg·m−2·h−1 and separation factor of 1127. Besides, there was no significant performance decline during the 160 h long-term stability test, indicating that the membranes own the potential application in industry.

ce:section.ce:para: The energy crisis has plagued humans for a long time, and the contribution of the separation and purification process is about 10–15% of the world's energy consumption [1]. Therefore, the efficient use of energy in the separation process has become an urgent need worldwide. Membrane separation technology has been preferred to meet the energy demand in many energy sectors because of its low energy consumption, pollution-free merit, simple operation, high selectivity, and easy scale-up. Hence, it is one of the most promising and economical separation methods.

Inorganic membranes have superior chemical and thermal stability compared to organic membranes that make them more prominent in water purification, gas separation, and chemical separation in harsh environment [2]. In addition, Ceramic membranes have extensive applications in petrochemical, mining, oil/water separation, textile, pharmaceutical and biological separation, and food industries. However, the applications of ceramic membranes are limited currently owing to the long production cycle and high production cost [3]. Due to the large pore size (hundreds or thousands of nm) of ceramic support, it is not feasible for molecular separation tasks, such as nanofiltration (NF), reverse osmosis (RO), forward osmosis (FO), pervaporation (PV), and gas separation.

The interfacial polymerization (IP) process has been used widely for the preparation of ultra-thin selective layer [4–7]. However, due to the rough surface and large pore size of the ceramic substrate, it is difficult to prepare the polyamide (PA) layer directly. That necessitates the presence of a multi-layer composite structure to decrease the pore size. Most of the selective layers prepared by the IP process involve polymers as an interlayer, while other configurations, through coating and sintering, use inorganic nanoparticles as the interlayer [8]. For the inorganic interlayer, the repeated coating and sintering process greatly increases the energy consumption, production cycle, and manufacturing cost. However, some inherent advantages of the inorganic substrate may be sacrificed for the organic interlayer, causing plasticization, swelling, or thermal degradation [8].

As a hydrophilic polymer, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is a good choice for an additive in the preparation of composite membranes. Tan et al. prepared a highly water-permeable PA separation layer on the polysulfone (PSF) support by adding PVA to inhibit the diffusion rate of ammonia monomer [9]. The introduction of PVA in the separation layer could also improve the hydrophilicity of membranes and facilitate the transfer of water [10]. Besides, PVA was also used as an interlayer. Zhu et al. employed a PVA interlayer to prepare an ultra-thin PA layer with a thickness of 9.6 nm on a PES UF membrane support [11]. However, PVA applications in inorganic ceramic membranes to assist the direct IP on macroporous ceramic membranes have not yet been reported.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) as emerging zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials had attracted extensive attention due to their unique fluorescence characteristics, extra small size, low toxicity, abundantly raw, and inexpensive cost [12–14]. These made CDs prominent to exhibit specific catalysis features, bioluminescence tomography, and chemical sensing [15–17]. In addition, their surfaces contain many hydrophilic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which show strong dispersibility in water [18]. Moreover, they also provided the feasibility for advanced functional group alteration and surface passivation [19]. In this way, CDs were considered nano-fillers with great potential for thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane preparation [19–22]. Zhang et al. doped CDs into the PA layer by an IP process and obtained a smoother, more hydrophilic, negatively charged surface, which makes it emerge 1.5 times the pure water flux during low-pressure NF for dye separation [23]. Wang et al. utilized the nanometer scale and super hydrophilicity of CDs, which provided shorter and less circuitous channels for the PV ethanol (EtOH) dehydration process, achieving a 60% increase in flux [24].

The separation layer with high selectivity and permeability was successfully prepared by direct IP process on the macroporous CHF substrate in this work. NH2 modified CDs (N-CDs) and PVA were employed as aqueous additives in the IP process. The preparation technology with huge advantages in low-energy and rapid preparation has been designed and explained at the molecular scale. The effects of PVA and CDs in the IP process were explored through the surface morphology, hydrophilicity, element composition, and functional group analysis of the required membrane. Besides, a long-term stability test was carried out to investigate the stability in practical application. The PV dehydration performance, energy consumption, and production cycle were compared with the state-of-the-art literature. The molecular dynamic (MD) simulation was used to build the diffusion model of the IP process. Besides, the density functional theory (DFT) was employed to calculate the interaction energy between the additives and EDA.

Citric acid (CA) was supplied by Aladdin (Shanghai). Trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98%) was provided by Qingdao Benzo Chemical Company (China). Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, K90) and polyethersulfone (PES, MW = 58 kDa) were obtained from BASF company. EDA, PVA (1750), and n-hexane were analytical grades and provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All reagents were used without extra purification. Lab-made deionized (DI) water was used in this study.

The porous α-Al2O3 ceramic hollow fiber (CHF) membranes with a pore size of about 300 nm were fabricated through the non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS) process followed by sintering treatment. Detailed procedures for preparing the CHF have been described previously [25].

The CHF pervaporation (CHFPV) membranes were fabricated on the outer surface of the CHF substrates by an IP process. Briefly, one end of the CHF substrate was sealed by epoxy and then dipped vertically for a specific time in the aqueous phase containing PVA and EDA. The drenched supports were dried in the air to without visible liquid on the surface. The fibers were immersed in the organic phase (0.5%, w/v TMC in n-hexane) for a specific time. After this, membranes were heated at 60 °C for 3 min to complete the IP reaction. The resultant membranes were hanged vertically in the air for 3 h to dry. The dried membranes were treated with methanol and dried again under ambient conditions. The fabricated CHFPV membranes were designated as PXCHFPV, where the superscript X represents the concentration of PVA.

N-CDs were synthesized through the partial carbonization of citric acid (CA) following the method reported by Zhu et al. [26]. In a typical synthesis: CA (1.00 g) and EDA (0.79 g) were dissolved in DI (10 mL). The solution was then transferred to a Teflon autoclave (30 mL) and heated at 150 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, a dialysis tube was used to purify the product.
The IP process was repeated as N-CDs added in the aqueous solution to prepare N-CDsYPCHFPV membranes, where Y represents the concentration of N-CDs (100, 300, 500, and 700 ppm).

Contact angle meter (JC2000A, Shanghai Zhong Cheng Digital Equipment Co., Ltd, China) was employed to measure the hydrophilicity of the membrane. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova Nano SEM 450) was used to observe the surface and section morphologies of membranes. The chemical composition and structure of the PA selective layer were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG Microlab II, UK). The functional groups of the N-CDs and selective layer were measured by the Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet-6700, America) with a scan range of 4000–500 cm−1. The Solution component content was analyzed by gas chromatography (GC, Techcomp GC7890T, China). The crystallinity of N-CDs was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Model D8 Advance Eco, Bruker) employing Cu K Î± radiation. Atomic force graphing microscopy (AFM, NanoScope IIIa Multimode Veeco) was used to characterize the surface roughness.

The resultant membranes were used to test the PV performance for 90/10 wt% EtOH/H2O mixture at 60 °C by lab-made apparatus [27]. The permeate was collected by a cold trap formed by liquid nitrogen as a cold source. EtOH and H2O content of the feed and permeate were analyzed from the GC. The operating temperature was systematically investigated in the range of 30–60 °C. The membranes were evaluated through three aspects, namely permeation flux (J), separation factor ( α ), and PV separation index (PSI). Further, the specific calculation formula is as follows:
(1)
J =
Q
A × t
(2)
α
EtOH / w a t e r
=
Y
EtOH
/
Y
H 2
O
X
EtOH
/
X
H 2
O
(3)
PSI = 1000 × J × ( α - 1 )
where Q is the mass (kg) of the permeate; A is the effective permeation area of CHF composite membrane (m2); t is the collecting time (h); Y and X are the mass ratios of water and EtOH in permeate and feed, respectively.

In order to further explore the essential effect of PVA and N-CDs on the IP process. The reaction mechanisms were trying to explain by MD simulation and DFT in this study. Furthermore, the detailed simulations were described in the Supplementary Information.

N-CDs were synthesized in advance, as shown in Fig. 1 . The morphology of the N-CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 2 a showed the uniform, monodisperse, and spherical shape of the synthesized N-CDs. In addition, the particle size distribution and mean diameter of N-CDs are 1–3 nm and 2 nm, respectively (Fig. 2b) [28]. The chemical structure and composition of N-CDs were characterized by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2c, the typical adsorption peaks at 3463, 1700, and 1642 cm−1 were ascribed to OH (hydroxyl group) stretching vibration, CO (carbonyl group) stretching vibration, and CC respectively [24,29]. The characteristic peaks at 1561 and 1081 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of NH and CN of the amino group in N-CDs [13]. These absorption peaks indicated the existence of amino-group and oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the as-synthesized N-CDs. According to the XRD spectra (Fig. 2d), a wide peak appeared at 2θ = 25° corresponding to a d-spacing of ~0.34 nm, showing a similar interlayer space of graphene [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of an aqueous solution for IP
ce:section.ce:para: The macroporous ceramic substrate with a large pore size (~300 nm) was challenging to direct IP. Because the aqueous phase can easily penetrate the pores, which will eventually form an incomplete PA layer (Fig. 3 ). Therefore, to prepare a defect-free separation layer, it is primary to ensure that the aqueous liquid film can be evenly distributed on the surface. First, PVA was added as an initial aqueous phase additive. On the one hand, it can reduce the surface tension of the solution and make it easier to spread. On the other hand, it can increase the viscosity of the solution and reduce the uneven distribution caused by gravity during the CHF IP process. However, a defect-free separation layer only formed at a relatively high PVA concentration (0.5 wt%), which would affect the permeation flux. N-CDs were used to form a network structure with lower concentrations of PVA, and reduce the diffusion rate of EDA to form a thinner PA layer [31].

MD simulations were carried out on three aqueous phase systems of EDA (E), PVA/EDA (P/E), and N-CDs/PVA/EDA(N-C/P/E). The original model of the N-C/P/E system was constructed, as shown in Fig. 4 b, and others were shown in (Supplementary Fig. S5). The density number distribution of EDA in the z-axis direction at different times were shown in Fig. 4a. and (Supplementary Fig. S6). N-CDs and PVA significantly affected the EDA content in the organic phase, from 62.57% to 27.73%. It reveals that the diffusion of EDA was notably suppressed. The diffusion coefficient of EDA under E, P/E, N-C/P/E system were
( 4.58 ± 0.50 )
×
10
- 5
c m
2
/ s
,
( 2.44 ± 0.47 )
×
10
- 5
c m
2
/ s
,
( 1.44 ± 0.10 )
×
10
- 5
c m
2
/ s
, respectively (Fig. 4c) [32]. Moreover, the details for other components were listed in (Supplementary Figs. S7 and S8). The electrostatic potential (ESP) results showed that N-CDs have a wider distribution than PVA, which means more interaction sites and stronger interaction force (Supplementary Figs. S1 and S4) [33]. The Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) were calculated further to investigate the interaction location (Supplementary Fig. S2). Compared with PVA, N-CDs showed stronger interaction with EDA, calculated by DFT theory (Supplementary Fig. S3).

SEM images of surface and cross-section morphologies for CHFPV, PCHFPV, and N-CDsPCHFPV membranes were shown in Fig. 5 . The membranes were immersed in liquid nitrogen for at least 3 min before they were shear fractured to obtain a flat section. Since the ceramic support and the PA layer exhibit different material shrinkage at polar temperatures, resulting the polyamide layer to peel off [27]. It can be obviously observed from the cross-section of Fig. 5b that the PA layer was very thick, discontinuous, and not dense. The uneven distribution of EDA may be due to rough surfaces and large pores of ceramic support. The scattered distribution of C elements in the EDX spectrum (Supplementary Fig. S9) proved that the PA layer was formed in zones instead of a complete layer.

The surface and cross-sectional morphologies of PCHFPV membranes were shown in Fig. 5c, d. When the PVA concentration was low, P0.1CHFPV still showed a thick selective separation layer and partial structural defects. It may be due to the defective PA layer allowing EDA to penetrate and react with TMC. When the PVA concentration reaches 0.5% (w/v), the PA layer was successfully produced. It indicated that it is feasible to prepare a defect-free selective separation layer by direct IP on the macroporous ceramic substrate.

As reflected by Fig. 5e, f, the addition of N-CDs further changed the morphology of the PA layer. Compared to P0.1CHFPV, N-CDs100P0.1CHFPV and N-CDs500P0.1CHFPV showed an ultra-thin, defect-free PA layer with 39 ± 5 nm and 28 ± 3 nm thickness. The thinner PA layer was formed probably due to the following reason. Less EDA penetrated through the PA germ layer due to inhibited EDA diffusion (Fig. 4). It is beneficial to prevent the PA layer from generating thicker. In addition, it presents a rougher and more wrinkled peak and valley structure after incorporating N-CDs. (More information about surface roughness provided by AFM in Fig. S10 and Table S2).

FT-IR spectra of CHFPV, PCHFPV, and N-CDsPCHFPV membranes were illustrated in Fig. 6 . The characteristic peaks located at 3277 cm−1, 1540 cm−1, and 1647 cm−1 correspond to OH (hydroxyl group), NH (amine Ⅱ) plane-bending vibration, and CO (amine Ⅰ) stretching vibration, respectively [34,35]. This proved that the PA layer was successfully formed on the membrane surface. Compared with CHFPV, P0.5CHFPV exhibited no noticeable difference except a stronger OH absorption peak. The characteristic peaks of CDs were not observed on the incorporated membranes. This may be caused by the insufficient loading and the characteristic peaks being covered by amide bonds. Similar phenomena occurred when Zhi et al. [36] incorporated 0.15% (w/v) GO in the PA layer or Sun et al. [13] incorporated CDs in the PA layer.

XPS was further used to explore the incorporation of N-CDs on the membrane. The O/N ratio indicated the degree of cross-linking of PA prepared by the IP process. In general, the lower the O/N ratio, the higher will be the cross-linking degree [37]. However, it is based on the completion of the PA layer not to be affected by the substrate. CHFPV and P0.1CHFPV membranes exhibit an unusually high O/N ratio, which was not only determined by the cross-linking, but the Al2O3 support was exposed by the damaged PA layer. The signal peak at 74.1 eV corresponds Al2p also proved that the PA layer was defective. The high-resolution C 1s spectra of the three PA layers surface were shown in Fig. 7 . The C 1s spectra of CHFPV and P0.1CHFPV consist of three signal peaks, corresponding to the CC/CO bond at 284.7 eV, the CN/CO bond at 286.0 eV, and the OCO/OCN bond at 287.6 eV [38]. Specifically, for the C 1s spectrum of N-CDs500P0.1CHFPV, a new signal peak appears at 288.5, which corresponds to the OCO of carbon quantum dots [39]. It proved the successful introduction of carbon quantum dots into the PA matrix.

The dynamic contact angles of CHFPV, PCHFPV, and N-CDsPCHFPV were shown in Fig. 8 . Compared with CHFPV, PCHFPV exhibited a higher initial water contact angle, and droplets disappeared entirely later. It may be due to the thin PA layer of the CHFPV membrane and tiny defects, making it easier for water to permeate through the PA layer. Furthermore, N-CDsPCHFPV showed a lower water contact angle and a shorter time for the droplets to disappear completely compared to PCHFPV. Three possible reasons can be explained for that. First, super-hydrophilic N-CDs introduce more hydrophilic groups such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl for the PA separation layer. Enhanced hydrogen bond interaction between the membrane surface and water molecules pushes the liquid–solid contact line to move forward [40]. Second, N-CDsPCHFPV exhibits higher surface roughness, so it has a larger actual surface area per unit projected area, and thus more hydrophilic groups also make it exhibit a smaller water contact angle. Third, micropores provided by CDs may also make water droplets disappear faster. The super-hydrophilicity of the ceramic substrate enables the aqueous solution to be thoroughly wet on the surface, and the aqueous monomer was evenly distributed on the surface, which provides a prerequisite for the preparation of an ultra-thin PA layer with uniform structure by an IP process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EtOH dehydration performance of N-CDsPCHFPV nanocomposite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of PVA concentration on EtOH dehydration PV performance was shown in Fig. 9 . It can be clearly seen that the presence or absence of PVA had a significant impact on performance. The experimental errors in Figs. 9 and 10 were caused by inherent differences between ceramic substrates parallel test, instead of multiple measurements. It can be concluded that PVA was helpful to reduce the difference of substrates on PV performance. For P0CHFPV membrane without the PVA additive, the quiet instability and relatively low separation effect were apparent. This result can also be easily inferred from the SEM images. Defects and cracks in large amounts exist in the active layer, which can hardly be selective to EtOH and water. It resulted in a large separation flux and a low separation effect. With the increase of PVA concentration, the separation factor shows an increasing trend, while the flux is continuously decreasing. It may be due to the presence of PVA made EDA more dispersed on the surface instead of quickly penetrating the substrate. The diffusion-inhibited EDA formed a complete PA layer instead of forming a fragmented PA layer on the rough macroporous support.

The dissolution-diffusion model is a widely accepted model used to explain the mass transfer process of PV. Solubility selectivity and diffusion selectivity determine the separation performance (Eq. (4)) [41]. The hydrophilicity of the surface improved the solubility balance of water molecules in the PA layer. With the addition of N-CDs, it showed a similar separation factor at minimum (100 ppm) and maximum (500 ppm) concentration of PVA and maintained the high flux level (Fig. 9). It was attributed to the modified structure constructed by PVA and N-CDs. PVA and N-CDs formed hydrogen bonds and woven into a network covering the CHF support surface, providing many attachment sites for the aqueous monomer EDA. The super-hydrophilic surface gave the water an advantage in the dissolution competition with EtOH and improved the water selectivity of the dissolution process. The corrugated surface provided a large separation area with the same hollow fiber surface area, which further enhanced the water dissolved and improved the permeation flux. For the diffusion process, the interlayer structure of N-CDs provided more diffusion channels for water molecules, thus improving the diffusion rate [42]. A thinner PA separation layer provided a lower mass transfer resistance for the PV, which made a higher permeation flux. In the case of defect-free PA layer, the separation factor has no obvious correlation with the membrane thickness. However, if defects and cracks in large amounts existed in the active layer, which can hardly be selective to EtOH and water. The feed quickly penetrated the permeate side from the defect. This also explained that PCHFPV membrane had a thick selective separation layer, but had high flux and low rejection. The factors mentioned above mutually affected the separation performance of PV. Finally, it showed the best separation effect in N-CDs500P0.1CHFPV, with a total permeation flux of 3.15 kg·m−2·h−1 and a separation factor of 1127. When the content of N-CDs continued to increase, the total permeation flux decreased slightly, and the separation factor hardly changed.
(4)
α
i / j
=
S i
S j
D i
D j
where
S i
is solubility;
D i
is diffusivity;
S i
/
S j
represents solubility selectivity and
D i
/
D j
represents diffusivity selectivity. (i, j are H2O and EtOH, respectively)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of operating conditions on EtOH dehydration performance
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs500P0.1CHFPV membrane was used to study the effect of operating temperature on the PV dehydration performance. The experiment employed 90 wt% EtOH aqueous solution as the feed, and the test temperature range was 30–60 °C. As shown in Fig. 10a, as the temperature increases, the total permeation flux increased significantly, but the separation factor slightly decreased. The reasons were as follows: the increase in temperature increased the differential vapor pressure between the two sides of the PA layer, which acts as the driving force for PV and increases the total permeation flux [43]. On the other hand, the higher temperature made the polymer molecular chain more mobile, which promoted the penetration of H2O and EtOH. Besides, more EtOH molecules permeated through the polymer molecular chain gap at high temperatures.

The Arrhenius equation (Eq. (5)) was used to describe the relationship between temperature and permeation flux quantitatively. Took the value of 1000/T as the x-axis variable, the ln(J) value as the y-axis variable to draw Fig. 10c. According to fitting results, the activation energy of alcohol (
E
a , E t O H
) and water (
E
a , w a t e r
) were 48.22 kJ·mol−1 and 37.5 kJ·mol−1, respectively. The
E
a, water
was greater than
E
a, EtOH
, indicating that EtOH was more sensitive to temperature changes than water. It also explained why the separation factor shows a downward trend as the operating temperature rises. The permeation flux of each component changes with temperatures was shown in Fig. 10b. With increasing temperature, the overall change trend of separation factor and flux was stable, which showed that the PA active layer has always remained intact, without damage or defects. It indicated the membrane thermal stability under variable temperature conditions.
(5)
J i
= A
e
- E
a , i
/ R T
where
J i
represents the permeation flux of component i ; A is the pre-exponential factor, R is the ideal gas constant, T is the absolute temperature of the feed liquid, and
E
a, i
stands for the apparent activation energy of component i.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PV performance of N-CDsPCHFPV membrane in long-term operation
ce:section.ce:para: The long-term stability of the membrane was particularly vital for industrial production applications. NCDs500P0.1CHFPV exhibited the relatively highest separation factor and permeation flux. Therefore, it was employed for long-term PV test with 90/10 wt% EtOH/H2O mixture at 60 °C for 160 h (Fig. 11 ). The flux remained virtually unchanged, while the separation factor fluctuated in the range of 1000 to 1400. It demonstrated the N-CDsPCHFPV membranes possess strong mechanical and chemical stability for EtOH dehydration for 160 h long-term test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of EtOH dehydration performance with state-of-the-art literature
ce:section.ce:para:
Comparison of the PV performance with state-of-the-art literature was presented in Fig. 12
[24,44–51]. N-CDsPCHFPV membranes exhibit excellent separation performance and permeation flux. Compared with the ceramic composite membranes, the separation factor was further improved at the same flux level. This is because N-CDs and PVA as aqueous phase additives induce a hydrophilic and denser PA layer formation. Compared with PA selective layer membranes, it had more advantages in permeate flux. It is attributed to the ultra-thin PA layer, interlayer free structure. Besides, macropore ceramic substrate provided the ultra-low mass transfer resistance for the penetration process. To sum up, based on macroporous ceramic substrates, the novel IP method has shown tremendous potential for the preparation of composite membranes. It has shown outstanding accomplishment in PV performance. Furthermore, it is suitable for industrial applications due to the energy-saving, short-production-cycle, low-cost.

In this study, amino (NH2) modified carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) doped TFN membrane was prepared on the macroporous ceramic hollow fiber (CHF) substrate through one-step interfacial polymerization (IP) process. The resultant N-CDsPCHFPV membrane tested with EtOH/H2O showed a high throughput of 3.15 kg·m−2·h−1 and a separation factor of 1127. The effects of PVA concentration and N-CDs loading on the surface morphology, hydrophilicity, and chemical structure of the membranes were systematically studied. The excellent performance of the N-CDsPCHFPV membrane demonstrates that the defect-free PA layer can be prepared on macroporous ceramic support by the IP process. The mass transfer mechanism of the IP process was studied by MD simulation. In addition, the interaction energy of additives and EDA was further explored by the DFT calculation. It provides an approach to investigate the formation process of the ultra-thin PA layer at a molecule scale. At inference, this IP process will expand the applications of the macroporous ceramic substrates in the manufacturing of NF, FO, and RO membranes. Furthermore, it is of great significance in reducing the production cycle and the cost of ceramic composite membranes. Moreover, a series of problems such as swelling was avoided due to the absence of a polymer interlayer.

Yu-Zhe Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Usman Shareef: . Ji-Peng Xu: . Zhen-Liang Xu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Ping-Ping Li: Investigation. Yu-Xuan Li: Investigation. Ping Li: Investigation. Peng Gao: Investigation. Xin Zhang: Investigation. Sun-Jie Xu: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the research funding provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22078092) and supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JKA012022001 and JKA012011017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2021.118572.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 1
